_note: Starter draft. Editorial voice and source attributions are intentionally light — fill in primary-source URLs (NN/g, Google Search Central, WCAG, schema.org, web.dev) when polishing. Slugs MUST match existing scaffolded considerations in the DB; the loader merges subs onto whatever already exists.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Define the one dimension that makes this a collection — category, taxonomy, search, time, status. Two dimensions = two pages.
sub_considerations.body: If you find yourself writing "products that are red AND on sale AND in stock" as the collection's identity, the page is doing two jobs. Pick the primary dimension for the route, leave the rest to filters. Mixed-dimension collections are a frequent cause of empty-state confusion and dilute SEO targeting.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Decide whether the user's job here is to discover, compare, or filter to a known item. The default sort follows from that answer.
sub_considerations.body: Discovery wants relevance or curation; comparison wants dense rows and price-asc; known-item-filtering wants exact match and instant filter feedback. Build for one — the others can still work, but pick the priority.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Show the most useful order first, not the most recent. "Newest" wins only for time-sensitive collections.
sub_considerations.body: A newest-first default fits news, releases, and feeds; almost nothing else. For browsing or buying, relevance (curated, by popularity, or by a business signal) beats chronology — most users never sort.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Make the default sort visible and changeable from one click — never hidden behind a menu.
sub_considerations.body: Users won't change the sort if they can't see it. Render the current sort as a labelled control near the top of the list, with the alternatives one tap away.

sub_considerations.one_liner: The title is the collection's dimension, not the brand or section. "Espresso machines under $500" beats "Shop · Espresso machines".
sub_considerations.body: The dimension is what the user came for. Brand and section live in the chrome (logo, breadcrumb) — repeating them in the H1 wastes SERP real estate and reading attention.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Skip the marketing intro on list pages. One sentence of context if it adds something, otherwise none.
sub_considerations.body: Intro paragraphs on collection pages push the list below the fold and rarely help users decide. If the dimension needs explaining, a short caption under the H1 is plenty.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Show the count of currently-visible results, not the total catalog. Filtered counts answer "did my filter work?".
sub_considerations.body: If 4 filters are applied and you still show "3,204 products", the user has no signal that anything narrowed. Display the filtered count, optionally with the total in parentheses.

sub_considerations.one_liner: When zero, don't just say "0 results" — name the applied filters and suggest which to relax.
sub_considerations.body: An honest zero-state tells the user what's blocking the list: "No espresso machines under $50 — try widening to $200, or change the Brand filter". This converts an apparent dead-end into a recoverable step.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Cards favour recognition; rows favour density. A 12-item card grid and a 50-row table are different products.
sub_considerations.body: Cards work when the visual matters (products, people, articles) and the user is scanning for one to click. Rows work when the user is comparing tabular attributes (price, status, date) across many items. Pick one — hybrids are usually worst of both.

sub_considerations.one_liner: One primary visual, one primary line, one secondary line. Anything more is noise.
sub_considerations.body: The card or row is a scannable unit, not a summary. Resist the pull to cram in ratings, badges, tags, dates, and CTAs all at once — pick the two or three signals that drive the next click.

sub_considerations.one_liner: The entire card is the link target. Inner buttons (favourite, quick-add) need e.stopPropagation().
sub_considerations.body: Users click anywhere on a card. A linked title with non-clickable whitespace around it is a usability paper-cut. Wrap the card content in an <a> and intercept the click on any inner controls so they don't navigate.

considerations.title: Pagination vs infinite scroll vs load-more

sub_considerations.one_liner: Pagination if users need to find a specific result later — URLs survive. Infinite scroll only for engagement-shaped browse.
sub_considerations.body: Paginated URLs (?page=3) can be shared, bookmarked, and crawled. Infinite scroll loses all three. Use it deliberately for feed-style experiences where position doesn't matter; default to pagination otherwise.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Load-more is the safe default — keeps URL semantics, supports keyboard, doesn't trap focus.
sub_considerations.body: A "Load more" button gives the engagement curve of infinite scroll without the accessibility tax: keyboard users can advance deterministically, focus stays predictable, and the page is still bookmarkable at any point.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Show how many remain. "Showing 1–24 of 312" beats a bare Next button.
sub_considerations.body: Counts let users decide whether to keep scrolling. Without them, advancing feels like a coin flip — "is there one more page, or twenty?". Render the visible-range and total alongside the pager.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Empty states explain why and what to try next. Never just "No results."
sub_considerations.body: Three jobs for the empty state: name what you searched/filtered for, suggest a concrete next step (widen this filter, try this search), and offer an escape (back to the unfiltered list). "No results" alone is a dead end.

sub_considerations.one_liner: First-load empty (no items in the collection at all) needs a different message than filtered-empty. Don't reuse.
sub_considerations.body: An empty collection on first load is a content problem ("this category is being built"); an empty filtered view is a refinement problem ("too narrow"). Reusing one copy for both is a frequent regression.

sub_considerations.one_liner: One filter per dimension; collapse the rest. A sidebar with 30 controls fails everyone.
sub_considerations.body: Filters compete for attention. Surface the two or three that drive most refinements and tuck the rest into a "More filters" overflow. Counts under each option help users see which are populated before clicking.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Selected facets persist in the URL. Refreshing or sharing must restore state.
sub_considerations.body: Filter state belongs in query parameters, not just client memory. A user who refreshes, shares, or back-buttons should land on the same filtered view. SEO benefits when canonical handling is correct (see crawlability).

sub_considerations.one_liner: Disable facets that would return zero, but keep them visible — the user may unselect what's blocking.
sub_considerations.body: Hiding zero-count options removes the affordance to recover; the user can't see why their filter narrowed to nothing. Show the option, dim it, optionally annotate with "(0)". One-click recovery beats a vanishing UI.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Sort is a dropdown, not a row of tabs. Tabs imply mutually-exclusive content; sorts don't.
sub_considerations.body: Tabs change WHAT is shown; sort changes the ORDER of the same thing. Using tabs for sort confuses users into thinking they're switching collections. A labelled select control is the boring, correct affordance.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Newest, price asc, price desc, rating, alphabetical — pick at most four. More is paralysis.
sub_considerations.body: Every additional sort option is one more decision the user has to consider. Pick the four that account for >95% of real use, and drop the rest. Custom sorts ("by popularity in your area") count as one.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Active filters render as removable chips above the list. The user must be able to undo without going back into the filter panel.
sub_considerations.body: The journey "add filter → see list → realise one filter is wrong → undo" should be one click on the chip, not a round-trip to the sidebar. Each chip names the dimension and value ("Brand: Sage", "Price: under $500") and has an explicit close affordance.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Clear All only when more than one filter is on. A single active filter doesn't need a sibling "clear all".
sub_considerations.body: Showing "Clear all" next to one chip is visually noisy and redundant — the chip itself clears. The control earns its space once two or more filters compound.

sub_considerations.one_liner: rel="prev"/rel="next" is no longer a Google ranking signal. Rely on internal links + canonical instead.
sub_considerations.body: Google deprecated rel=prev/next as a pagination signal in 2019. Internal links between paginated pages (Next/Prev buttons) plus correct canonicals are now the supported pattern. Don't noindex page 2+ by default — that strands the link equity.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Don't canonicalise every filter combination as a separate URL. Use one canonical for the unfiltered list.
sub_considerations.body: Filter permutations (color=red&size=xl&brand=…) explode into hundreds of near-duplicate URLs. Set the canonical to the unfiltered list URL so search engines consolidate signals; let users still bookmark filtered URLs for sharing.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Render the first page server-side. Defer below-the-fold images with loading="lazy" — never on the first row.
sub_considerations.body: The first paginated page is the page most users see; serving it client-rendered taxes LCP for everyone. Hydrate progressively if needed, but the initial HTML should contain real items.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Above ~1,000 rendered items, virtualise the DOM. Below ~100, just paginate.
sub_considerations.body: Virtualisation (only rendering the visible window of rows) pays off when DOM size starts hurting scroll performance. For typical e-commerce or article lists, pagination at 24–48 per page is simpler and avoids virtualisation's accessibility footguns.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Use Schema.org ItemList with itemListElement entries pointing to each detail page's URL.
sub_considerations.body: Each list entry should reference the detail page rather than inline the whole object. Type-specific subclasses (Product, Article, RealEstateListing, JobPosting) give richer SERPs than a bare ItemList. Pair with numberOfItems for the current visible count.

sub_considerations.one_liner: The list type must match the rendered semantics. Don't mark unranked tag clouds as ordered ItemLists.
sub_considerations.body: If your list isn't ranked, use itemListOrder: Unordered (or omit it). Marking a homepage's "trending tags" cloud as an ordered ItemList misleads search engines and can result in awkward rich-result snippets.
